Here's another demonstration for you. I like this one. This is the common enemy demonstration. This is a demonstration that was actually used for in a restaurant, Roy Fatt used it. It's his birthday, so he's having a special customer appreciation event. Here's the next one. This one is the mailing that I did a couple of years ago, where Karen and I, we took a trip through a couple of different countries. And so what I did was, I bought postcards while I was away and then I just, all I did was take the cards and duplicated like, you know, 15,000 of them. And when I got back, I mailed them out over a course of days, day two, day six, day 12. So the first place we went to was Ireland. So I sent out a postcard from Ireland, life in the fast lane. Okay. And on the backside of the card, there was a whole message. Then six days later, I said, we were at Gibraltar. So thinking of you, when I got this postcard, a whole message. And then a couple of days later, we were in Barcelona, Barcelona where girls just want to have fun. And we sent that out on day 12. Okay. And we sent it out a series of days. Step number one is the survey your list. Step number two is to report back results to them of the survey. And step number three is to give them what they want, which in this particular case, the results in this case is easier to things that I wanted people to speak at, at the seminar. One site selection where you're doing it at. What are the what are the big things to think about. Number one thing is location. Dan is 100 percent right. It's best to keep people isolated. My favorite place to have an event that not because it's my favorite city. Although it is one of my favorite cities. But my favorite place to have a seminar is actually Chicago because it's about a two and a half hour flight from anywhere in the United States. It's easy to get to. There's two big airports there. Always lots of flights coming in and coming out. So it's my favorite place to have it. But you can't have it there every time. So so we you know we move them around. So before we come to any event we always have a group of folks that we actually give the bid out to and show them a complete list of all the equipment that we need. So you want to do that as well because there's great diversity, great range that you can actually find it would people will charge you for that. Discount from vendors for booking multiple events. So and the AV folks will usually give you discounts for multiple events. The hotels will give you discounts for multiple events. Marriott Sheraton Hyatt Hilton's. If you say to them I'm doing three events in the next two years. Well they'll give you even better discounts for multiple events. So those are some vendor tips. OK, so that was the dreaded stuff. I will tell you in terms of a hotel contract like this going back and forth. Sherry going back and forth with them over and over back and forth back and forth. It probably takes us to negotiate the final lives of contract. Usually around six weeks with all the back and forth. So it's not like you just get the hotel contract and you sign it. If you get the first hotel that contract that they if you sign the first hotel contract that they delivered to you and you sign it and send it back to them. What they do is they get their entire staff in a room and they all start laughing at you. OK, so what you got to do is understand that they are testing you to see how much of this stuff you actually know. OK, seminar choreography to maximize profitability. So this is now choreographing the event. The event that you're similar to an event you're sitting in right now or any event you go to. You have to look at it as choreographing it. This is probably the single biggest thing that I do with people that I consult with in the seminar business where I help them to actually put on really very, very productive and profitable events. We always have a big session, never less than an hour to actually figure out how to choreograph the entire event. So here's the things we think about. Number one is making sure you deliver great value as soon as possible. Deliver great value as soon as possible. What are the first who's the first speaker here yesterday? Dan Kennedy. Did he deliver great value? How many people, how many of you, well, I want to ask, don't raise your hand. Many of you walked up to me after Sam Dan's presentation and said, if I just go home right now, I'm done. Most of you who didn't walk up to me were thinking that. OK, great value after that. Cal Ripken Jr. Mary Ellen Tribby. I mean, great value early right away to deliver great value. So all of a sudden people said to themselves, I made the right decision to come here. So you got to deliver right up the bat. Great value as soon as possible. Number two, you got to make the list. You're number one. You're number two. Number three, chief objectives of the seminar for the host. You got to make the list of what your three top objectives are. And you've got to now choreograph the event to put number one first, number two, second, number three, third, as far as what you want to do. It is not a goal of a Glaser Kenny event, but a lot of people that I work with in niche markets, the biggest thing that they want to, their biggest objective is to sell coaching programs at their events. Obviously, it's not a goal at Glaser Kenny events because we don't sell a lot of coaching programs. Even my Platinum program that I offered earlier today is only for some of you. It's not for all of you. So it's not our chief goal. But a lot of events, the number one thing they want to sell is coaching at their events. That's the number one thing. So when we choreograph the whole agenda, it's all built around selling that thing. And then everything else takes a backseat to it, number two takes a backseat to it, second, number three, third, diversity, non duplication of topics. This thing is crazy. Okay. You wouldn't believe how many times speakers complained to me that they go to events and speak at it. And there's two other people on the agenda talking about the same thing they're talking about. It's crazy. So you can't have any duplication of topics. Number four is speaker introductions. Speaker introductions. How the speaker is introduced makes a gigantic difference in the choreography of the event. Most of us that are serious about putting on events, we always introduce the speakers ourselves. Chris, where's Chris? Chris Cardell is in Guinness Book of Records for being the best speaker, introducer of all times. He's got the book of records for that. He does the best introductions because Dan and I flew over to the UK. They never heard of Bill Glazer and Dan Cain in the UK enough. Okay. And by the time he was done introducing us, we were kind of famous over there. You know, so speaker introductions are really, really important how you introduce the speaker. And this also ties into being a person of influence and also being, having credibility and believability with your attendees. But the speaker and the introducer being the person of influence because he can transfer that influence over to now the speaker. The introducer, the host, can do that. Speakers being respectful of other speakers. This is, those of you in the room, how many of you like do a fair amount of speaking? A lot of speaking. Okay. So you like know, you probably, you had an incidence on this where you've spoken on the stage and other speakers have tried to sabotage you. You know, we had one speaker years ago, some of you were probably there, was the most sabotaging speaker of all times I've ever seen. He has never been back on a Glacier Kennedy stage since that time. Really big sabotage. He'll say, he'll try to do anything at all to hurt the other speakers. So making sure speakers are being respectful of other speakers. Have you seen that at this event where you're at where all the speakers were respectful of everybody else, everybody finished on time? Did you see all that? Nobody going over all this, nobody talking badly about anybody. I won't even tolerate it, you know, at our stage. And it's important that that is thought about. If you don't know about that, you can get sabotaged by your speakers. If the speaker is offering a resource, God forbid, okay, you have to think about who speaks before the speaker, who speaks after the speaker, this one, when the order forms are distributed, when the, how the order forms are collected, what the speaker's average closing percentage is elsewhere, and how speakers resources affect your number one, number two, and number three objective. So if you bring in a speaker, well, that speaker's resource affect your main three objectives of what you're trying to accomplish at your events. So all that has to be thought out in the choreography of the events. Position the speakers to keep attendance throughout the seminar. So if you have, if you don't have speakers that are speaking throughout the seminar to keep attendance, so let me tell you a little bit of inside baseball. Do you know why Frank Kern is speaking tomorrow and not the first day? That is because he's positioning away because everybody wants to hear Frank Kern speak. And he's positioning away, everybody will be here tomorrow morning to hear Frank Kern speak on the third day of the event. Why are we giving away the Cal Ripken jersey and all the gifts at the end? That's all about bringing people back. So I'm being totally transparent to you all. So you can think worse of me now or later, but it doesn't matter. But I'm telling you, it's all about keeping people for attendance. General session versus breakout session topics. So my rule of thumb on that usually is, is a general session topic should be interesting to everybody in the room. And a breakout session should be specialized where people can choose which ones they want to go to. And there's a real important purpose for both. But you also have to think of that in terms of do you want to have general sessions or breakout sessions? Okay. Then there's the secret sauce. The secret sauce to these events, and Dan, I was happy to see you spoke about this yesterday, is moving from a seminar to a unique experience. See, these are no longer seminars. They really have to be unique experiences. So the GCAT has the event unique experience. Networking events for certain levels of membership. Special meal functions with certain levels of membership. Book signings with the authors. Meet and greet and fellow opportunities with celebrities. Contests and competitions. Award ceremonies. Prize giveaways. Chair massage therapists. VIP lounge for certain levels of membership. Special Q and A sessions with certain attendees. Exhibitors as resources. Speakers resources to facilitate fast growth. So actually offering speakers resources to facilitate fast growth. So the people that we allow to come here and offer you a resource, it's not just because we want to sell you stuff. It's because of the fact we want to, once we show you that you need this as something that you would help you in your business and you make that decision whether this is helpful in your business, you can then invest in something to get you moving fast in it. So like for example, let's use Stephen Beck's webinar program. Once you realize that I want to do better webinars or I want to do webinars, his thing is all designed in a way to get you doing that faster and getting you to do them better. So it's really valuable to have resources and if you don't have, it's actually, it's actually un-valuable if you are able to show people the opportunity but then not give them the resources they need to implement the opportunity. So whether or not you have those, of course a lot of thought has to be given into that. So like for example, here is an agenda. So this is what we, this is an internal agenda. You probably would not see something like this as far as how we choreograph our entire events that we use in our seminars. So we have the registrations the night before, Thursday, Thursday, then it moves along to Friday. There's breakout sessions that are over there and then we go back to general session and then that's that. So did I go too fast? Oh, no I didn't. And then over here, I think this is then the bonus day. This is actually the bonus day we had at the 2010 Super Conference, which was the greatest bonus day we've ever had all the time. We did the big showcase of people competing for the title. And more people have talked, given me great feedback about that than anything else we have done, which is why we're doing it again next year with our members.
